title: Polyglot Persistence
body:
# Polyglot Persistence

## Hook — a motivating scenario

One app needs: transactional order data (relational), a product catalog with flexible attributes
(document), a session store (key-value), full-text product search (search engine), a recommendation
graph (graph DB), metrics (time-series), and big media files (object storage). Forcing all of that into
one database means every workload fights the others. **Polyglot persistence** says: use **multiple,
purpose-fit data stores**, each for the job it's best at — and accept the operational cost that brings.

## Mental model — the right database for each data shape

**Polyglot persistence** is using **different data stores for different needs within one system**,
chosen by **data shape and access pattern** rather than forcing one database to do everything. It's the
storage-level version of "right tool for the job," built on everything you've learned about how stores
differ (SQL vs NoSQL, the NoSQL families, TSDBs, object storage, search, graph).

```match
{
  "prompt": "Match each workload to the store best suited to it.",
  "pairs": [
    { "left": "Transactional orders/payments (need ACID)", "right": "Relational DB (PostgreSQL/MySQL)" },
    { "left": "Sessions / hot key-value lookups", "right": "Key-value store (Redis)" },
    { "left": "Full-text product search", "right": "Search engine (Elasticsearch)" },
    { "left": "Recommendation / social graph", "right": "Graph DB (Neo4j)" },
    { "left": "Metrics / IoT time-series", "right": "Time-series DB (Prometheus/Influx)" },
    { "left": "Large media files", "right": "Object storage (S3)" }
  ]
}
```

## Build it up — the benefit and the very real cost

The benefit is clear: each store is **optimized** for its workload, so you avoid the compromises of a
single general-purpose database (a relational DB doing full-text search badly, a TSDB doing
transactions, etc.).

But polyglot persistence has a steep **operational and consistency cost**:
- **Operational complexity:** each store is another system to deploy, monitor, back up, secure, scale,
  patch, and *develop expertise in*. Five datastores = five sets of failure modes and on-call
  knowledge.
- **Consistency across stores:** the same entity now lives in multiple places (order in SQL, indexed in
  Elasticsearch, cached in Redis), and there's **no transaction across them** (recall dual-write,
  outbox, CQRS) — keeping them in sync is **eventual** and your responsibility (events/CDC).
- **Cognitive load:** developers must know several data models and their quirks.

```reveal
{
  "prompt": "What's the hidden cost teams underestimate when adopting polyglot persistence, and how do you keep multiple stores in sync?",
  "answer": "The underestimated cost is the operational and consistency burden of running many stores rather than the appeal of each being optimal. Each datastore you add is a full system to operate: deployment, scaling, backups and tested restores, security/patching, monitoring/alerting, capacity planning, upgrades, and — critically — team expertise in its data model, failure modes, and tuning. Five specialized stores mean five sets of on-call knowledge and five things that can break differently; this dwarfs the cost of the happy-path query benefits and is why teams often consolidate. The second hidden cost is cross-store consistency: the same entity is now duplicated across stores (e.g. an order in the relational DB, indexed in Elasticsearch, cached in Redis, summarized in analytics), and there is no ACID transaction spanning them, so the naive 'write to DB then write to search/cache' is the dual-write problem (a failure between writes leaves them inconsistent). You keep them in sync the same way CQRS read models are kept in sync: make one store the source of truth and propagate changes asynchronously via reliable events — typically the transactional outbox (write the change + an event atomically in the source DB) or change-data-capture (CDC, e.g. Debezium tailing the DB log), with idempotent consumers updating the other stores. This yields eventual consistency across stores (the search index/cache lag the DB briefly), which you design for (read-your-writes handling where needed). So the realistic picture: polyglot persistence trades single-store simplicity for per-workload optimality, paying in multiplied operational complexity and self-managed eventual consistency across stores — adopt it only when a workload genuinely needs a different store, keep a clear source of truth, and sync via outbox/CDC + idempotent projectors rather than ad-hoc dual writes."
}
```

## Build it up — when it's worth it (and avoiding sprawl)

- **Worth it** when a workload genuinely needs a different store — search, graph traversal, time-series
  ingest, blobs, hot key-value — that your primary DB serves poorly. Then a specialized store is a big
  win.
- **Avoid sprawl:** don't add a store for marginal benefit. Each one multiplies cost, so prefer
  consolidating where one store is "good enough" (e.g. Postgres can do JSON documents, full-text
  search, and even time-series via extensions — often avoiding a separate store). The **right number**
  of stores is the **fewest** that adequately serve your distinct workloads, not the most "optimal" per
  workload.
- **Designate a source of truth** per entity and **sync derived stores** via events/CDC (recall
  outbox/CQRS) — don't dual-write.

```reveal
{
  "prompt": "Polyglot persistence says 'use the optimal store per workload' — so why is 'optimal store for every workload' often the wrong goal?",
  "answer": "Because optimality per workload ignores the dominant real-world cost: each additional store multiplies operational complexity (deploy/scale/backup/secure/monitor/patch + required expertise and distinct failure modes) and cross-store consistency burden (duplicated data, no cross-store transactions, eventual sync to maintain). Chasing the theoretically-best store for every workload leads to datastore sprawl — many systems to run, each a source of on-call pain and a sync challenge — whose total cost easily exceeds the marginal query-performance gains. Often a single capable store is 'good enough' for several workloads: e.g. PostgreSQL handles relational data, JSONB documents, full-text search, geospatial, and (via TimescaleDB) time-series, so you might serve catalog, search, and metrics adequately without adding a document DB, a search engine, and a TSDB — dramatically less to operate. The right objective is therefore the FEWEST stores that adequately meet your genuinely-distinct needs, not the most specialized per need. You add a specialized store only when a workload truly suffers in your primary store (e.g. heavy graph traversal, large-scale search, high-ingest metrics, big blobs) and the benefit clearly outweighs the added operational + consistency cost. So polyglot persistence is 'use a different store when a workload genuinely needs it,' not 'use the perfect store for everything' — pragmatic consolidation beats theoretical per-workload optimality, because the costs are paid in operations and consistency, not just in query plans."
}
```

Adopting polyglot persistence is really a dial on how many stores you run — slide from consolidation to specialization:

```tradeoff
{
  "title": "How many data stores should one system run?",
  "axis": { "left": "Consolidate (fewest stores)", "right": "Specialize (optimal store per workload)" },
  "steps": [
    { "label": "One capable primary", "detail": "Use a single store like Postgres for relational, JSON documents, full-text search, geo, and time-series via extensions. Lowest operational and consistency burden; query performance is 'good enough', not optimal." },
    { "label": "Add a store when one workload truly suffers", "detail": "Keep the primary, but split off a workload it serves poorly (e.g. heavy search to Elasticsearch). Each addition multiplies ops cost and needs cross-store sync via outbox/CDC." },
    { "label": "Several purpose-fit stores", "detail": "Relational + Redis + Elasticsearch + S3 + TSDB, each optimized for its workload. Strong per-workload performance, but five sets of failure modes and self-managed eventual consistency." },
    { "label": "Optimal store for every workload", "detail": "Maximal specialization. Best theoretical per-query performance, but datastore sprawl: most systems to operate, most on-call knowledge, most cross-store sync to maintain — often net worse." }
  ]
}
```

## In the wild

- **Virtually every large system is polyglot:** e.g. PostgreSQL (orders) + Redis (sessions/cache) +
  Elasticsearch (search) + S3 (media) + a TSDB (metrics) + maybe a graph DB.
- **Sync via events/CDC + the transactional outbox** (recall) keeps derived stores (search index,
  cache, analytics) consistent with the source of truth — eventually.
- **CQRS** is closely related: read models often live in different stores than the write model (recall).
- **Consolidation trend:** "just use Postgres" (with extensions for JSON/search/geo/time-series) is a
  popular counter-movement to avoid sprawl.

## Common misconception — "use the best database for every single thing"

The cost is **operational + consistency**, so the goal is the **fewest stores that suffice**, not the
most specialized.

```reveal
{
  "prompt": "Why can adopting many specialized databases make a system worse overall, even if each store is individually 'best' for its workload?",
  "answer": "Because system quality is dominated by total operational and consistency cost, not by the sum of per-workload query optimality. Every specialized store you add is another production system to deploy, scale, secure, patch, back up (and test restores), monitor/alert on, and — crucially — staff with real expertise; each has distinct failure modes and on-call burdens. Run five or six and you've multiplied complexity, increased the surface area for outages, and stretched the team thin, often outweighing the modest performance gains each specialized store provides over a 'good enough' general store. On top of that, data gets duplicated across stores with no cross-store transactions, so you inherit dual-write/consistency problems and must build and operate reliable eventual-sync (outbox/CDC + idempotent projectors), adding more moving parts and subtle bugs (stale search index, cache divergence). So a system with many 'individually optimal' stores can be slower to develop, harder to operate, and more failure-prone than one with fewer, adequately-capable stores — net worse despite local optimality. The pragmatic principle is to minimize the number of stores: use one capable primary (e.g. Postgres, which covers relational + JSON + search + geo + time-series via extensions) for as much as it serves well, and add a specialized store only when a workload genuinely needs it and the benefit clearly exceeds the added operational + consistency cost. Optimize the whole system (operability, consistency, team focus), not each workload in isolation."
}
```

**Polyglot persistence** = using **multiple purpose-fit stores** chosen by **data shape/access
pattern**, each optimal for its workload — but it adds **operational complexity** and **cross-store
consistency** burden (no cross-store transactions; sync via **outbox/CDC**). Aim for the **fewest
stores that suffice** (consolidate where one is good enough), not the most specialized.

## Self-test

```quiz
{
  "question": "Polyglot persistence means:",
  "options": [
    "Writing the app in multiple programming languages",
    "Using different data stores for different workloads within one system, chosen by data shape/access pattern",
    "Replicating one database many times",
    "Storing everything in a single NoSQL database"
  ],
  "answer": 1,
  "explanation": "It's 'right store for each job' — relational for transactions, search engine for search, TSDB for metrics, etc. — within one system."
}
```

```quiz
{
  "question": "The main cost of polyglot persistence is:",
  "options": [
    "Slower queries everywhere",
    "Operational complexity (many systems to run) + cross-store consistency (no cross-store transactions; sync via outbox/CDC)",
    "It can't use caching",
    "It requires a single leader"
  ],
  "answer": 1,
  "explanation": "Each store adds ops/expertise burden, and duplicated data across stores must be kept eventually consistent (outbox/CDC), with no cross-store ACID."
}
```

## Recap — key terms

Flip each card to check yourself, then move through the deck:

```flashcards
{ "title": "Polyglot persistence — key terms", "cards": [
  { "front": "Polyglot persistence", "back": "Using multiple, purpose-fit data stores within one system, each chosen by data shape and access pattern rather than forcing one database to do everything." },
  { "front": "Main benefit", "back": "Each store is optimized for its workload, so you avoid the compromises of a single general-purpose database (e.g. a relational DB doing full-text search badly)." },
  { "front": "Main cost", "back": "Operational complexity (each store is another system to deploy, monitor, back up, secure, scale, patch, and gain expertise in) plus cross-store consistency burden." },
  { "front": "Cross-store consistency problem", "back": "The same entity lives in multiple stores with no transaction across them, so keeping them in sync is eventual and your responsibility." },
  { "front": "How to sync derived stores", "back": "Designate a source of truth per entity and propagate changes asynchronously via events/CDC or the transactional outbox with idempotent consumers — not ad-hoc dual writes." },
  { "front": "Right number of stores", "back": "The fewest that adequately serve your distinct workloads — consolidate where one store is good enough — not the most specialized per workload." }
] }
```

## Key takeaways

- **Polyglot persistence** uses **multiple purpose-fit data stores** (relational, key-value, search,
  graph, time-series, object) chosen by **data shape and access pattern**.
- Benefit: each store is **optimized** for its workload, avoiding one DB's compromises.
- Cost: **operational complexity** (more systems to run/know) + **cross-store consistency** (no
  cross-store transactions → sync via **events/CDC/outbox**, eventual).
- Aim for the **fewest stores that adequately serve distinct workloads** (consolidate where one is good
  enough), with a clear **source of truth** per entity — not maximal specialization.

## Up next

A related pattern for splitting one logical database across systems/domains. Next: **Database
Federation**.
